content.body:
Plant growth hormones (phytohormones):

Auxin (IAA): promotes cell elongation
в†’ Produced at shoot tip
в†’ Phototropism: light side = less auxin в†’ less elongation; dark side = more auxin в†’ bends toward light
в†’ Gravitropism: roots grow down (auxin inhibits root cells at high concentration)

Gibberellins: stem elongation, seed germination, fruit development
Cytokinins: cell division, delay senescence
Abscisic acid (ABA): dormancy, stomatal closure
Ethylene: fruit ripening, leaf abscission

content.title: Tropisms
content.body:
Phototropism: response to light
в†’ Positive (shoots grow toward light)
в†’ Negative (roots grow away)

Geotropism/gravitropism: response to gravity
в†’ Positive (roots grow downward)
в†’ Negative (shoots grow upward)

Thigmotropism: response to touch (e.g. climbing plants, tendrils)
Chemotropism: response to chemicals (e.g. pollen tubes)

content.title: Commercial Applications
content.body:
Auxin (rooting powder): cutting dipped in auxin в†’ stimulates root growth
Gibberellin: makes seedless grapes larger, breaks seed dormancy
Ethylene: ripens bananas (applied as gas in storage), abscission
Cytokinins: tissue culture, delay ripening in cut flowers
ABA: induces seed dormancy, drought response (closes stomata)

questions.question: Auxin promotes growth by
options: stimulating cell elongation (water enters cells by osmosis)
questions.answer: stimulating cell elongation (water enters cells by osmosis)
questions.explanation: Auxin: loosens cell walls в†’ cells elongate as they absorb water. Controls differential growth in tropisms

questions.question: In phototropism, a shoot bends toward light because
options: auxin redistributes to shaded side в†’ more elongation there в†’ bends toward light
questions.answer: auxin redistributes to shaded side в†’ more elongation there в†’ bends toward light
questions.explanation: Darwin showed tip responds to light, causing redistribution. Shaded side gets more auxin в†’ more elongation в†’ bending toward light

questions.question: Roots show positive gravitropism meaning
questions.answer: they grow downward (toward gravity)
questions.explanation: Positive gravitropism: response in direction of stimulus. Roots: high auxin concentration on lower side INHIBITS root growth в†’ roots curve downward

questions.question: Abscisic acid (ABA) causes stomata to
questions.answer: close (guard cells lose water)
questions.explanation: ABA: stress hormone produced during drought в†’ triggers ion/water loss from guard cells в†’ stomata close в†’ reduces transpiration

questions.question: Ethylene gas is used commercially to
questions.answer: ripen fruit uniformly in transit/storage
questions.explanation: Ethylene (Cв‚‚Hв‚„) triggers ripening: softening, colour change, sugar increase. Fruits stored in low Oв‚‚/high COв‚‚ to slow ethylene production

questions.question: Auxin at HIGH concentration in roots causes
options: less growth (inhibition) compared to low concentration
questions.answer: less growth (inhibition) compared to low concentration
questions.explanation: Roots are more sensitive to auxin than shoots. High auxin: inhibits root elongation. This explains why root tip grows DOWN (auxin accumulates below due to gravity, inhibiting lower side в†’ upper side grows more в†’ curves down)

questions.question: Rooting powder contains auxin because
options: it stimulates root initiation on stem cuttings
questions.answer: it stimulates root initiation on stem cuttings
questions.explanation: Auxin applied to cutting base в†’ dedifferentiation of callus cells в†’ new root formation в†’ cutting grows into independent plant

questions.question: Gibberellin treatment produces longer stems with larger fruit because
options: it promotes cell elongation and division in stem internodes and fruit cells
questions.answer: it promotes cell elongation and division in stem internodes and fruit cells
questions.explanation: Gibberellin: stimulates elongation of internodes and helps fruit grow larger. Commercially used on grapes, sugar cane

questions.question: Phototropism was investigated by Charles Darwin who showed
options: the shoot TIP detects light, signal sent to lower region where bending occurs
questions.answer: the shoot TIP detects light, signal sent to lower region where bending occurs
questions.explanation: Darwin's experiments (1880): covered tip в†’ no bending; tip exposed, lower region covered в†’ bending still occurred. Tip perceives, base responds

questions.question: Thigmotropism in climbing plants means
options: they respond to touch, wrapping tendrils around support
questions.answer: they respond to touch, wrapping tendrils around support
questions.explanation: Thigmotropism: differential growth in response to touch/contact. Tendrils of pea, cucumber wrap around supports вЂ” increases stability

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP вЂ” the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6COв‚‚ + 6Hв‚‚O + light energy в†’ Cв‚†Hв‚Ѓв‚‚Oв‚† + 6Oв‚‚. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient вЂ” no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body:
Homeostasis: maintaining stable internal environment

Key variables regulated:
вЂў Body temperature (37В°C in humans)
вЂў Blood glucose (4-6 mmol/L)
вЂў Blood pH (7.35-7.45)
вЂў Blood pressure
вЂў Water/salt balance (osmolarity ~300 mOsm/L)

Negative feedback principle:
Stimulus в†’ receptor в†’ control centre в†’ effector в†’ response (returns to set point)
Change detected в†’ corrective action OPPOSES the change

content.title: Thermoregulation in Humans
content.body:
Too hot:
вЂў Vasodilation: skin capillaries dilate в†’ more heat lost
вЂў Sweating: evaporation cools skin
вЂў Hairs lie flat: less insulation
вЂў Reduced metabolism

Too cold:
вЂў Vasoconstriction: skin capillaries constrict в†’ less heat lost
вЂў Shivering: muscle contractions generate heat
вЂў Hairs erect (piloerection): trap air insulation
вЂў Increased metabolism

Thermoreceptors in skin and hypothalamus detect temperature

content.title: Ectotherms vs Endotherms
content.body:
Endotherms (birds, mammals): generate heat metabolically, maintain constant body temperature
в†’ Advantages: active in cold, constant enzyme rates
в†’ Disadvantages: high energy cost

Ectotherms (reptiles, fish, amphibians): body temperature varies with environment
в†’ Advantages: low energy cost, efficient
в†’ Disadvantages: slow in cold, cannot survive extreme cold

questions.question: Homeostasis maintains
options: stable internal environment despite external changes
questions.answer: stable internal environment despite external changes
questions.explanation: Homeostasis: constant internal conditions (temperature, glucose, pH etc.) despite varying external environment and metabolic activity

questions.question: Negative feedback in homeostasis means
options: deviation from set point triggers corrective response that OPPOSES the change
questions.answer: deviation from set point triggers corrective response that OPPOSES the change
questions.explanation: Negative feedback: output opposes input. Body temp rises в†’ sweating cools в†’ temp falls back. Self-correcting system

questions.question: Vasodilation in skin during overheating causes
options: more blood to skin surface в†’ increased heat loss by radiation and convection
questions.answer: more blood to skin surface в†’ increased heat loss by radiation and convection
questions.explanation: Vasodilation: wider capillaries в†’ more blood flow to skin в†’ more heat to surface в†’ more radiation/convection to environment

questions.question: Shivering generates heat because
options: rapid, involuntary muscle contractions increase metabolic rate releasing heat
questions.answer: rapid, involuntary muscle contractions increase metabolic rate releasing heat
questions.explanation: Shivering: fast muscle contractions в†’ ATP hydrolysis в†’ heat released. Can increase metabolic rate 5Г— above resting

questions.question: Hypothermia occurs when body temperature drops below approximately
questions.answer: 35В°C
questions.explanation: Hypothermia: core temperature below 35В°C. Below 32В°C: unconscious. Below 28В°C: cardiac arrest risk

questions.question: A reptile on a cold morning moves slowly because
options: its ectothermic body is cold, reducing enzyme activity and metabolic rate
questions.answer: its ectothermic body is cold, reducing enzyme activity and metabolic rate
questions.explanation: Ectotherms: body temp = environment temp. Cold morning в†’ slow chemical reactions в†’ sluggish movement

questions.question: Hypothalamus acts as the thermostat because it
options: contains thermoreceptors that detect blood temperature and coordinates thermoregulatory responses
questions.answer: contains thermoreceptors that detect blood temperature and coordinates thermoregulatory responses
questions.explanation: Hypothalamus: temperature regulatory centre. Detects blood temp changes в†’ sends signals to skin, muscles, blood vessels

questions.question: Endotherms maintain constant temperature at high energy cost. Main advantage over ectotherms is
options: can remain active and maintain optimal enzyme rates in cold environments
questions.answer: can remain active and maintain optimal enzyme rates in cold environments
questions.explanation: Endothermy: constant enzyme/metabolic rates regardless of external temperature в†’ active at dawn, at altitude, in cold regions

questions.question: Fever (pyrexia) during infection is
options: an adaptive response вЂ” higher temperature inhibits bacterial growth and enhances immune function
questions.answer: an adaptive response вЂ” higher temperature inhibits bacterial growth and enhances immune function
questions.explanation: Moderate fever: set point elevated by pyrogens в†’ higher body temp в†’ reduces bacterial growth rate в†’ immune cells more active

questions.question: Piloerection (goosebumps/raised hairs) in cold helps by
options: trapping layer of insulating air close to skin
questions.answer: trapping layer of insulating air close to skin
questions.explanation: Erector pili muscles raise hairs в†’ air trapped в†’ insulating layer reduces heat loss. Less effective in humans (little body hair) than in furry mammals

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP вЂ” the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient вЂ” no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Coordination. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Coordination concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Coordination builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 3.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Coordination. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 3.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Coordination. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Coordination include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Coordination. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP вЂ” the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6COв‚‚ + 6Hв‚‚O + light energy в†’ Cв‚†Hв‚Ѓв‚‚Oв‚† + 6Oв‚‚. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient вЂ” no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: Cв‚†Hв‚Ѓв‚‚Oв‚† + 6Oв‚‚ в†’ 6COв‚‚ + 6Hв‚‚O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow COв‚‚ in and Oв‚‚ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (COв‚‚)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb COв‚‚ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass в†’ Rabbit в†’ Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient вЂ” no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: Cв‚†Hв‚Ѓв‚‚Oв‚† + 6Oв‚‚ в†’ 6COв‚‚ + 6Hв‚‚O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow COв‚‚ in and Oв‚‚ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (COв‚‚)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb COв‚‚ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass в†’ Rabbit в†’ Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Coordination. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Coordination concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Coordination builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Coordination. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Coordination. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Coordination include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Coordination. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP вЂ” the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6COв‚‚ + 6Hв‚‚O + light energy в†’ Cв‚†Hв‚Ѓв‚‚Oв‚† + 6Oв‚‚. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.
